title: Growth Regimes in the United States since 1920: Structural Breaks, Fiscal Structure, and the Productivity-Pay Divergence
description: A reproducible empirical analysis of long-run United States real GDP from 1920 onward. Annual growth is characterised as a sequence of statistically identified regimes rather than a single trend. Log GDP is shown to be empirically indistinguishable from a unit-root process while its growth rate is stationary; regimes are detected by dynamic-programming segmentation with a recursive within-segment refinement, and their uncertainty is quantified with parametric-bootstrap structural-break tests, break-date confidence intervals, and a robustness grid. The analysis integrates federal fiscal structure, the dynamic response of growth to narrative tax-regime shocks estimated by local projections, and the divergence between real GDP per capita and real median earnings. Every reported statistic is generated programmatically from the model outputs.
